recommendationReason: Genuinely opens NeRF to scattering media via a physics-grounded rendering equation, with quantitative gains over MIP-NeRF360, NeRF-W, and NeRFReN on real Red-Sea/Curaçao/Panama footage from a small lab.
rankingExplanation: PDF confirms strong novelty=19 (first principled NeRF-in-medium formulation). methodologicalRigor=17 from the derivation + ablations. experimentalQuality=15 — solid real-world results but the three-scene sample is small. authorInstitutionReputation=8 — University of Haifa + IUI Eilat, deliberately not a flagship roster, which is the F4 anti-pattern signal. Total 71 → RECOMMEND, confirming unknown-author papers can clear the floor.
summary: SeaThru-NeRF integrates the SeaThru wideband image-formation model into NeRF rendering, decomposing each ray into separate object and medium components so the scene and the scattering/absorbing medium are jointly recovered, enabling photorealistic novel-view synthesis and medium-free reconstruction in real underwater scenes.
keyContribution: A NeRF rendering model in scattering media that splits each ray's color into object and medium contributions with separate per-color-channel medium parameters (σ^attn, σ^bs, c^med), enabling restoration of the clear scene and recovery of medium properties from posed underwater images.
methodologySummary: Starts from NeRF's volume-rendering equation and the SeaThru underwater image-formation model, derives discretized rendering equations that splits transmittance into 'attenuation' (T_i^obj × exp(-σ^attn s_i)) and 'backscatter' branches with different medium densities. The architecture has a position-only MLP for object density/color and a direction-only Medium MLP for medium parameters; training adds a propagation prior and a Laplacian-mixture occupancy prior to L_recon.
strengths: Physics-grounded ray model is more principled than prior NeRF-W / NeRFReN extensions for underwater appearance — Eq. 22's medium decomposition is directly tied to the SeaThru image formation model.

Quantitative comparison on three real underwater scenes (Red Sea, Curaçao, Panama) shows ~0.8 dB PSNR improvement over MIP-NeRF360 on novel views, with stronger gains in 'far' regions where scattering dominates.
Ablations on number of medium parameters (1 vs. 3 vs. 6) and rendering-equation choice (intermediate Eq. 13–14 vs. final Eq. 22) directly support the architectural decisions.
Even on synthetic clean (LLFF Fern in air) the method is competitive with MIP-NeRF360 baseline (Fig. 4), showing the medium model degrades gracefully when no medium is present.
Code, data, and dataset of underwater scenes released — substantive contribution to a niche but real domain.
weaknesses: Only three real scenes (sample sizes 18–20 images each); broader evaluation across water types and depths would strengthen the empirical claim.
Camera poses extracted by Agisoft/SfM are unreliable in low-visibility water — the paper notes this but does not fully quantify the failure regime.
The model assumes opaque objects with σ^obj ≫ σ^med; semi-transparent or strongly forward-scattering objects (jellyfish, kelp) likely fail.
Limitation section acknowledges no transient effects (flickering, caustics) — these are common in real underwater scenes.
